questions.question: What does `{P} C {Q}` mean in Hoare logic?
options: If P holds before C and C terminates, Q holds after
P and Q are runtime logs
questions.explanation: Hoare triples express partial correctness: postcondition holds after terminating execution from valid preconditions.

questions.question: What role does the rule of composition play in Hoare logic?
options: It merges two programs into one
It chains proofs: if {P} C1 {Q} and {Q} C2 {R}, then {P} C1;C2 {R}
questions.explanation: The composition rule lets you build correctness proofs for sequential programs by matching the postcondition of one statement to the precondition of the next.

questions.question: Why is a loop invariant necessary?
options: To connect each iteration to the final postcondition
questions.explanation: Invariant preservation is the key bridge from loop body behavior to whole-loop correctness.

questions.question: What does partial correctness omit?
questions.explanation: Partial correctness assumes termination; proving total correctness also requires a termination argument.

questions.question: What is the weakest precondition of `x := x + 1` with postcondition `x > 5`?
questions.explanation: Working backwards, if `x + 1 > 5` must hold after assignment, then `x > 4` must hold before — this is the weakest precondition that guarantees the postcondition.
